coredata.dc:title: Stable and high-efficiency P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cells with a graphene quantum-dots down-converter
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Thakur, Diksha
Hsieh, Meng-Lin
Chiang, Shou-En
Yen, Yung-Sheng
Liu, Wei-Ren
Hsiung Chang, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: The photo-induced degradations in the photovoltaic performance of high-efficiency P3CT-Na based CH3NH3PbI3 (MAPbI3) solar cells were investigated by using a boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BG-QDs):poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) film coated glass substrate or a glass substrate as the light shielding window. With the use of a BG-QDs:PMMA film coated glass substrate, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell slightly decreased from 18.50% to 18.23% under one sun illumination for 20 min. To investigate the photo-induced changes in the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples with and without the shielding from a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate, the X-ray diffraction patterns, absorbance spectra and photoluminescence spectra are measured and analyzed. It shows that the blue and green photons can increase the defect density in the MAPbI3 thin film and thereby resulting in the irreversible photovoltaic performance. Fortunately, the BG-QDs:PMMA thin film is not only used to shield the high energy photons, but also can be used as an efficient down-converter and thereby resulting in a stable and high-efficiency perovskite solar cell.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo-induced degradation
MAPbI3 solar cells
Boron-doped graphene quantum dots film
Down-converter

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Current density–voltage curves of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell measured with the different shields under one sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2).

Forward and backward current density–voltage (J-V) curves of the fresh P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields: (a) without shield, (b) BG-QDs:PMMA/glass and (c) glass. (d) Difference in the current density between the forward and backward J-V curves.
Illumination-time dependent power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields under one sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2).

Forward and backward current density–voltage curves of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields under one sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2). Measurement time: (a) 5 min; (b) 42 min; (c) 64 min; (d) 86 min.

Forward and backward current density–voltage (J-V) curves of the aged P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields: (a) without shield, (b) BG-QDs:PMMA/glass and (c) glass. (d) Difference of the current density between the forward and backward J-V curves.
Incident photon-to-current conversion (IPCE) spectra of the aged P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields under one sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2).

X-ray diffraction patterns of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass before (after) the one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies: (a) blue and green photons, (b) red photons, (c) near-infrared photons and (d) one sun.

X-ray diffraction patterns of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples before (after) the one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies: (a) blue and green photons, (b) red photons, (c) near-infrared photons and (d) one sun. During the light illuminations, the samples are covered with a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate.

Absorbance spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies: (a) blue and green photons, (b) red photons, (c) near-infrared photons and (d) one sun.

Absorbance spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies: (a) blue and green photons, (b) red photons, (c) near-infrared photons and (d) one sun. During the light illuminations, the samples are covered with a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies: (a) blue and green photons, (b) red photons, (c) near-infrared photons and (d) one sun.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies: (a) blue and green photons, (b) red photons, (c) near-infrared photons and (d) one sun. During the light illuminations, the samples are covered with a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate.
Photovoltaic performance of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell measured with the different shields under one sun illumination (AM 15.G, 100 mW/cm2).

head.ce:title: Stable and high-efficiency P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cells with a graphene quantum-dots down-converter

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photo-induced degradations in the photovoltaic performance of high-efficiency P3CT-Na based CH3NH3PbI3 (MAPbI3) solar cells were investigated by using a boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BG-QDs):poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) film coated glass substrate or a glass substrate as the light shielding window. With the use of a BG-QDs:PMMA film coated glass substrate, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell slightly decreased from 18.50% to 18.23% under one sun illumination for 20 min. To investigate the photo-induced changes in the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples with and without the shielding from a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate, the X-ray diffraction patterns, absorbance spectra and photoluminescence spectra are measured and analyzed. It shows that the blue and green photons can increase the defect density in the MAPbI3 thin film and thereby resulting in the irreversible photovoltaic performance. Fortunately, the BG-QDs:PMMA thin film is not only used to shield the high energy photons, but also can be used as an efficient down-converter and thereby resulting in a stable and high-efficiency perovskite solar cell.

ce:section.ce:para: High-quality poly-crystalline CH3NH3PbI3 (MAPbI3) thin films can be fabricated with various solution processes under low-temperature fabrication conditions (Razza et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2016; Zhong et al., 2018), which has received a lot of attention in the field of optoelectronic devices such as solar cells (Kojima et al., 2009), light-emitting diodes (Tan et al., 2014); photodetectors (Sutherland et al., 2015) and X-ray detectors (Wei et al., 2016). MAPbI3 is a perovskite crystal, which is constructed by a PbI3 framework and MA cations via Van der Waals forces (Motta et al., 2015). It means that the orientation and rotation of organic cations highly influence the excitonic and electronic characteristics of perovskite crystals (Ren et al., 2016; Selig et al., 2017), which provides the superior light absorption and photo-carrier transportation abilities in perovskite crystals (Frost et al., 2014; Motta et al., 2015). In other words, low-bandgap organic–inorganic perovskite crystals (Quarti et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Prasanna et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020) are very suitable as the light absorbing material for converting photons to electric currents. In recent five years, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of solution-processed perovskite solar cells can be higher than 23% (Meng et al., 2018; Bi et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020) and 20% (Saliba et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019; Wan et al., 2020) by using the regular-type and inverted-type device architectures, respectively. Therefore, there is still room for improvement in the inverted-type perovskite solar cells. In inverted-type perovskite solar cells, hydrophilic PEDOT:PSS thin films were widely deposited on top of the ITO/glass substrates as the hole transport layer (HTL), which can provide a wide distribution in the PCE from 10% to 16% and the relatively low fill factors (FFs) from 55% to 70%. (Chang et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2016; Chang et al., 2017; Chang et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2018; Thakur et al., 2021) The sunlight-induced degradation in the photovoltaic performance of PEDOT:PSS based perovskite solar cells is mainly due to the increase in the carrier recombination in the perovskite layer. The FF and open-circuit voltage of inverted-typed perovskite solar cells can be increased by replacing the PEDOT:PSS thin films with a hydrophobic HTL, such as NiOx, CuOx, PTAA and thiophene based polymer thin films (Lin et al., 2020; Rao et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2015). The formation of solution processed perovskite thin films is highly related to the surface properties of the substrates. (Bi et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2017; Pylnev et al., 2021) In general, the higher hydrophobicity of the substrate results in the larger grain size of the perovskite thin film due to the lower number of perovskite nucleation sites, which can result in the photo-stable perovskite crystal thin films. Besides, the photovoltaic performance and stability of inverted-type perovksite solar cells can be improved via the modification of the electron transport layer. (Mohseni et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020) However, the photo-induced degradation mechanisms of hydrophobic HTL based perovskite solar cells are not completely understood yet.

The main goal of this study is to explore the photo-induced degradation mechanisms of the hydrophobic thiophene polymer based MAPbI3 solar cells with and without the shielding from a boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BG-QDs):poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) thin film. Our experimental results show that the blue and green photons can increase the formation of permanent defects in the MAPbI3 thin film and thereby resulting in the smaller shunt resistant, larger current hysteresis and lower short-circuit current density (JSC). In other worlds, the photo-induced degradation of the inverted-type MAPbI3 solar cells mainly originates from the formation of the additional defects in the MAPbI3 thin films, which is highly related to the photon energy.

BG-QDs are synthesized as following processes. Nitration reaction was carried out by using 6-g pyrene into 200-mL HNO3(aq) (18 M) under 220 rpm stirring at 85 °C for 24 h. Afterward, an ice bath was used to stop the reaction and 2-L de-ionized (DI) water was used to dilute the solution. Collect the material by filtration with a large amount of DI water until the pH = 5. Dry the material at 80 °C to obtain the GQDs. Take 0.5-g GQDs powder into a 100-mL Teflon tank, and then add 0.62-g H3BO3(s) and 30-mL DMF into the tank. Then, stir the solution for 30 min and ultrasonic vibration for 30 min to fully disperse the solutes in the solution. Put the solution to 100-mL autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 24 h. Separate impurities through suction and filtration. Finally, BG-QDs can be obtained. Figure S1 presents the transmission electron microscopic image and Raman scattering spectrum of the BG-QDs and the day-dependent photoluminescence intensity of the BG-QDs/ethyl acetate solution, which indicates that the single crystalline BG-QDs can be used as efficient down-converter. BG-QDs and PMMA are dissolved in dichlorobenzene (DCB) in order to prepare a BG-QDs:PMMA (0.1 wt%) film on top of the glass substrate by using the spin coating method. The concentration of BG-QDs:PMMA/DCB solution is 10 wt%. The spin rate and time for the formation of a BG-QDs:PMMA film are 500 rpm and 40 s, respectively. After the spin coating process, the sample was annealed at 100℃ for 10 min. Figure S2 presents the atomic-force microscopic image of the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass sample. The low surface roughness of 0.21 nm indicates that the scattering loss from the polymer thin film can be ignored.

P3CT-Na polymers are synthesized through the chemical interaction between poly(3-(4-carboxybutyl)thiophene) (P3CT) (regioregularity of 89%, Rieke) with NaOH powders (99.99%, Alfa Aesar) in a DI water solution. 9-mg P3CT and 1.947-mg NaOH were added into a 4.5-mL DI water and 0.987-mL DI water, respectively. The NaOH/water solution was slowly added into the P3CT/water solution. Then, the mixture of P3CT/water and NaOH/water was stirred at 40 ℃ for 6 h. MAPbI3 precursor solution was prepared by dissolving the CH3NH3I (MAI) crystals (0.2384 g) and PbI2 powders (0.6915 g) in a 1-mL dimethylformamide (DMF)/dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) mixture (9:1 v/v), which resulted in a 1.5-M MAPbI3 precursor solution. 10 mg phenyl-C61-butyric-acid-methyl-ester (PCBM) powder (99.5%, Uni-Onward) was dissolved in a 0.5-mL chlorobenzene (CB). 0.25-mg bathocuproine (BCP) powder (99%, Seedchem) was dissolved in a 0.5-mL isopropanol (IPA) (99.5%, ACROS). These solutions were stirred at 350 rpm for 4 h under room temperatures.

Before the deposition of the P3CT-Na thin films, the ITO/glass samples were treated by using UV-Ozone cleaner for 50 min in order to increase the surface hydrophilicity. The P3CT/water solutions were spin-coated on top of the ITO/glass samples with a post annealing process at 140 ℃ for 20 min. The spin rate and time of the P3CT-Na solution are 4000 rpm and 60 s, respectively. The MAPbI3 thin films were deposited on top of the P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples by using the spin-coating method with a washing-enhanced nucleation process (WEN) (Jeon et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2015; Chang et al., 2019). In the WEN process, the dropping time of antisolvent CB is the last 23 s. The 50 nm-thick PCBM thin films were spin-coated on top of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples. The BCP/IPA solution is used to increase the electron collection efficiency at the PCBM/MAPbI3 interface. Then, the 100-nm thick Ag films were deposited on top of the BCP:PCBM/MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples with low deposition rates (<0.5 nm/s). The active area of the solar cells is defined to be 2 × 5 mm2 with a steel shadow mask during the deposition process of Ag thin film. The energy diagram, device structure and preparation process of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cells are illustrated in Figure S3.

In the device characterization, the J-V curves of the four solar cells were measured by using a source-meter system simultaneously (NI-USB 6356 DAQ) under one sun illumination (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2). The wavelength-dependent J-V curves of the solar cells were measured from 420 nm to 850 nm at a fixed intensity of 4 mW/cm2. In addition, the light intensity of the light-emitting diodes based solar simulator (VeraSol-2, Newport) was calibrated by using a reference Si cell (91150 V, Newport),

In the material characterizations, the home-made absorbance spectrometer (Chiang et al., 2020), home-made photoluminescence spectrometer (Chang et al., 2020) and X-ray diffractometer (D2 Phaser, Bruker) were used to analyze the material properties of the BG-QDs film, BG-QDs dissolved in DCB and MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples.

Fig. 1 presents the current density–voltage (J-V) curves of the fresh P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell measured with the different shields. The related photovoltaic performance is listed in Table 1 . When a glass substrate is used as the shield, the decrease in the JSC is mainly due to the reflections from the two interfaces of the glass. When a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate is used as the down-converter, the JSC increases from 20.60 mA/cm2 to 22.90 mA/cm2 and thereby increasing the PCE from 16.73% to 17.79%, which means that the higher energy (shorter wavelength) photons can be effectively converted into lower energy (longer wavelength) photons by using the BG-QDs:PMMA film. Figure S4 presents the absorbance spectrum and the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate, which shows that the BG-QDs can convert the UV-to-yellow photons to red photons centered at the wavelength of 607 nm. Besides, the FF increases from 77.4% to 79.1% when a BG-QDs:PMMA film is used as the down-converter, which means that the non-radiative recombination of red photon-generated carriers is lower than that of green photon-generated carriers in the MAPbI3 thin film (Chen et al., 2015).

Fig. 2 presents the forward and backward J-V curves of the fresh P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields under one sun illumination. Without the use of a shield, the difference in the current density (ΔJ) between the forward and backward J-V curves is 0.21 mA/cm2. ΔJ can be used to evaluate the current hysteresis of solar cells and the defects in MAPbI3 thin films. (Li et al., 2021) ΔJ significantly increases from 0.21 mA/cm2 to 0.61 mA/cm2 when a glass is used as the shield, which means that the defects in the MAPbI3 thin film can be filled by sunlight-generated carriers and thereby decreasing the current hysteresis (Pazoki et al., 2017). ΔJ decreases from 0.61 mA/cm2 to 0.23 mA/cm2 when a BG-QDs:PMMA film is used as the down-converter, which means that defects of the MAPbI3 thin film are sensitive to the blue and green photons. In other words, the defects of the MAPbI3 thin film do not participate in the generation of photocurrents under red and/or near-infrared light illuminations.

To confirm the observation in Fig. 2, the illumination time-dependent PCE values of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields are plotted in Fig. 3 . Without the use of a shield, the PCE increases from 19.44% to 20.04% with the increase in the illumination time from 0 min to 5 min due to the photo-induced annealing effects (You et al., 2020). The initially changing rate of PCE is −10.0%/hr. After 35 min, the PCE decreases to a stable value of 17.12%. The PCE increases from 17.12% to 18.50% without one sun illumination for 30 s, which can be explained as due to the fast self-healing process in the MAPbI3 thin film (Nie et al., 2016). When a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate is used as the down-converter, the initially changing rate of PCE is −4.4%/hr. After 64 min, the PCE largely increases from 15.32% to 18.23% without one sun illumination for 30 s. The PCE slightly decreases from 18.50% to 18.23% when a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate is used as the down-converter during the sunlight illumination. When a glass plate is used as the shield, the initially changing rate of PCE is −7.1%/hr. After 86 min, the PCE slightly increases from 14.25% to 15.37% without one sun illumination for 30 s. The PCE largely decreases from 18.23% to 15.37% when a glass substrate is used as the shield during the sunlight illumination. Besides, the ΔJ increases from 0.23 mA/cm2 to 1.47 mA/cm2 with the increase in the illumination time, as shown in Fig. 4 . The trend of the PCE values is proportional to the trend of the ΔJ, which suggests that the decreased PCE is due to increased defects in the MAPbI3 thin film. Besides, the ΔJ only slightly increases from 0.59 mA/cm2 to 0.60 mA/cm2, which means that the illumination of red and near-infrared photons on the solar cell does not create the additional defects in the MAPbI3 thin film.

After the illumination time-dependent experiment, the forward and backward J-V curves of the aged P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields are measured again. It is noted that the JSC of the aged P3CT based MAPbI3 solar cell increases from 19.89 mA/cm2 to 21.99 mA/cm2 (see Fig. 4 (d) and Fig. 5 (a)) while keeping the VOC and FF, which is probably related to the changes at the PCBM/MAPbI3 and MAPbI3/P3CT-Na interfaces. In other words, the photo-induced phase separations at the PCBM/MAPbI3 and MAPbI3/P3CT-Na interfaces can be partially self-recovery at room temperatures. Without the use of a shield, the ΔJ of the aged solar cell is 0.50 mA/cm2. The ΔJ increases to 0.75 mA/cm2 when a glass substrate is used as the shield, which means that the defects of the MAPbI3 thin film can be partially filled by the sunlight-generated carriers. When a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate is used as the down-converter, the ΔJ decreases from 0.75 mA/cm2 to 0.37 mA/cm2. It means that sunlight illumination induced permanent defects in the MAPbI3 thin film do not significantly influence the generation of photocurrents by the red and near-infrared photons.

Fig. 6 presents the incident photon-to-current conversion (IPCE) spectra of the aged P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with the different shields. The ripples of the black line in the IPCE spectrum are related to the interference pattern of the ITO thin film. Compared with the black line, the IPCE values of the blue line are lower due to the optical reflection loss when a glass substrate is used as the shield. When a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate is used as the down-converter, the IPCE values are increased in the short wavelength range from 450 nm to 580 nm mainly due to the light absorption of the BG-QDs (see Figure S4). The IPCE spectra of the red and blue lines have the similar trend in the wavelength range from 580 nm to 780 nm, which can be explained due to the reduced reflection when the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass is used as the shield. The refractive index and thickness of the PMMA thin film are 1.49 (Beadie et al., 2015) and 150 nm, respectively. According to the thin-film interference effect (Chang et al., 2014), the 150-nm thick PMMA thin film can result in a lowest reflection value at the wavelength of 894 nm. Therefore, the transmittance (reflectance) values of the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate can be slightly higher (lower) than that of the glass substrate in the wavelength range from 580 nm to 780 nm. In addition, the trend of the IPCE spectra can be used to confirm the trend of the JSC values in Fig. 5.

To investigate the light-induced changes in the material properties of the MAPbI3 thin films, the XRD patterns, absorbance spectra and PL spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples are measured with and without the use of a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate. The spectra of the different illumination lights are plotted in Figure S5. The intensities of the illumination lights are fixed at 30 mW/cm2. Besides, the X-ray different patterns of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass sample are almost overlapped within four hours (see Figure S6), which confirms the formation of high-quality MAPbI3 crystal thin films. Fig. 7 presents the XRD patterns of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies. When the blue and green photons are used to illuminate the sample, the peak intensities both are decreased with one-hour illumination. (see Fig. 7 (a)). Besides, the additional peak of (004) at 28.7° and shoulder of (002) at 14.5° are formed after one-hour illumination with the blue and green photons. When the red (near-infrared) photons are used to illuminate the sample, the intensities of the two main diffraction peaks at (110) both are increased with one-hour illumination, which indicates that the crystallinities of the MAPbI3 thin films can be increased via the photo-induced thermal annealing effects (You et al., 2020). The red light and near-infrared light are effectively absorbed by the MAPbI3 thin film and ITO thin film, respectively, which results in the different photo-induced thermal annealing processes. The illumination of near-infrared photons increases the peak intensity ratio of (110) and (220) from 1.55 to 1.80, which means that the photo-induced thermal annealing of the ITO thin film releases the residual stresses at the ITO/P3CT-Na and P3CT-Na/MAPbI3 interfaces and thereby increasing the crystallinity of the MAPbI3 thin film while keeping the initial crystal structure (see Fig. 7(c)). When the sample is excited under one sun illumination for one hour, the decrease in the XRD intensity and the formation of the (004) peak are originated from the illumination of the blue and green photons. (see Fig. 7(d)).

Fig. 8 presents the XRD patterns of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies. When the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate covered sample is illuminated with the blue and green photons, red photons or one sun, the integrated intensity of the two main diffraction peaks is almost fixed while changing the crystal orientation of the MAPbI3 thin film. It means that the use of a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate can avoid the photo-induced degradation of MAPbI3 thin films (Joshi et al., 2016). Besides, the peak intensity of the main diffraction increases from 503 to 598 when the near-infrared photons are used to illuminate the sample, which confirms that the photo-induced heat treatment process from the ITO thin film can increase the crystallinity of the MAPbI3 thin films.

Fig. 9 presents the absorbance patterns of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies. The trend of the absorbance values in the blue and green wavelength ranges is proportional to the trend of the intensities of the main diffraction peak at (110) (see Fig. 7), which means that the better crystallinity of the MAPbI3 thin film results in the higher absorbance in the blue and green wavelength ranges. Fig. 10 presents the absorbance spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies. When the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate covered sample is illuminated with the blue and green photons or red photons, the absorbance values are decreased in the blue wavelength range. It shows that the absorption coefficient of (002)-oriented MAPbI3 crystal is lower than that of (110)-oriented MAPbI3 crystal from 350 nm to 450 nm. Besides, the increase in the intensity of the main diffraction peak (see Fig. 8(s)) does not result in the increase in the absorbance of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass in the short wavelength range from 350 nm to 450 nm (see Fig. 10(c)), which means that the P3CT-Na thin film contributes a negative response in the absorbance spectrum due to the thermal induced packing of polymers (Jo et al., 2019). When the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate covered sample is illuminated with the sunlight, the formation of dual oriented MAPbI3 crystals (see Fig. 8(d)) and the enhanced absorbance in the second absorption band (see Fig. 10(d)) means that the sunlight-treated MAPbI3 thin film consists of multi-crystalline grains (Chen et al., 2016).

Fig. 11 presents the PL spectra of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass samples before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies. When the sample is illuminated with the blue and green photons or red photons, the PL intensity is largely increased (see Fig. 11(a) and (b)). The PL intensity from the MAPbI3 thin film can be used to evaluate the contact quality at the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na interface because the photo-generated holes in the MAPbI3 thin film can be collected by the p-type P3CT-Na polymer thin film. In other words, the increase in the PL intensity of the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na/ITO/glass sample indicates the decrease in the contact quality at the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na interface. It is noted that the shallow defects of the MAPbI3 thin film can be formed after one-hour light illumination with the blue and green photons (see Fig. 11(a)). When the sample is illuminated with the near-infrared photons, the PL intensity is decreased (see Fig. 11 (c)). It indicates that the contact quality at the MAPbI3/P3CT-Na interface can be increased by heating the free carriers in the ITO thin film with the near-infrared photons (Chen et al., 2016). When the sample is illuminated with sunlight, the PL intensity is increased (see Fig. 11 (d)). The increased PL intensity can be used to explain the decreased JSC of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell without the use of a shield after one sun illumination for 40 min (see Fig. 4(a) and (b)). Fig. 12 presents the PL spectra of the BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate covered samples before (after) one-hour light illumination with the different photon energies. The PL intensities are slightly increased after the different illuminations, which can be used to explain the minor reduction in the JSC of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell with a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass substrate after one sun illumination for 20 min (see Fig. 4 (b) and (c)).

In summary, we have investigated the photo-induced degradations of the high-efficiency P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell measured without and with the use of a boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BG-QDs) as the down-converter. The experimental results show that the crystal orientation, light-harvesting ability and exciton dynamics of the MAPbI3 thin films deposited on top of the P3CT-Na/ITO/glass substrates are highly sensitive to the different excitation wavelengths. The irreversible defects of the MAPbI3 thin film can be formed after one-hour illumination with the blue and green photons, which can be used to explain the sunlight-induced degradation of the P3CT-Na based MAPbI3 solar cell measured without the use of a shield. Fortunately, the use of a BG-QDs:PMMA/glass down-converter can result in a photo-stable and high-efficiency MAPbI3 solar cell, which means that it is possible to replace Si based solar cells by using perovskite solar cells in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology under Grant MOST 107-2112-M-033-001-MY3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2021.07.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Achieving reproducible and high-efficiency (>21%) perovskite solar cells with a presynthesized FAPbI3 powder

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, S. Seo, S. Y. Lim, Y. Kim, S.-G. Kim, D.-L. Lee, S.-H. Lee, H. Shin, H. Cheong, N.-G. Park, Achieving reproducible and high-efficiency (>21%) perovskite solar cells with a presynthesized FAPbI3 powder, ACS Energy Lett. 5 (2020) 360-366.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhong et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blade-coated hybrid perovskite solar cells with efficiency > 17%: An in situ investigation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhong, R. Munir, J. Li, M.-C. Tang, M. R. Niazi, D.-M. Smilgies, K. Zhao, A. Amassian, Blade-coated hybrid perovskite solar cells with efficiency > 17%: An in situ investigation, ACS Energy Lett. 3 (2018) 1078-1085.
